channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Suspect in Kansas City Shootings Found Dead in Basement, Police Say
item.description: Officers found the body of a man believed to be Oscar Sanchez-Munoz, the suspect in a series of roadway shootings last week, inside a home they had already searched.
item.dc:creator: Madaleine Rubin

item.title: After Trump’s Outburst, Senate G.O.P. Reverses Course on Iran
item.description: In a late-night vote aimed at mollifying the president, Senate Republicans rejected a resolution directing him to end the war against Iran, a day after a bipartisan rebuke.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Michael Gold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump traveled to the Capitol for a tense closed-door lunch with Senate Republicans on Wednesday.

item.title: Venezuelans in South Florida Call Relatives and Organize Aid
item.description: The large Venezuelan community in the Miami area is “desperate” to reach family members.
item.dc:creator: David Ovalle
item.media:credit: Manaure Quintero/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Wreckage and a reunion on a street in Caracas on Wednesday.

item.title: Ex-Youth Pastor Charged With Wife’s Murder, 20 Years After Her Fatal Fall
item.description: Bernadette Vander Meer’s death in Zion National Park in Utah had been considered an accident. Prosecutors reopened the case in 2022 and now say her husband was having an affair with an underage girl.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: TBA for The New York Times
item.media:description: The view from the summit of the Angels Landing Trail in Zion National Park, where Bernadette Vander Meer died in 2006.

item.title: Judge Throws Out Federal Suit Against 4 N.J. ‘Sanctuary’ Cities
item.description: Judge Evelyn Padin ruled the Justice Department’s lawsuit ​was flawed because it did not also challenge a state directive limiting local cooperation with federal immigration agents.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
item.media:description: Newark police officers at a protest outside Delaney Hall this month.

item.title: NATO’s Leader Makes His Case to Trump for Preserving the Alliance
item.description: Using charts, Secretary General Mark Rutte appeared to defuse the president’s anger by showing that European countries were “equalizing” defense spending with the U.S.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mark Rutte, the secretary general of NATO, with President Trump and other officials in the Oval Office on Wednesday. The organization’s summit starts on July 7 in Turkey.

item.title: Dolly Parton’s Fans Were Worried. She Came to a Truck Stop to See Them.
item.description: Fans have been concerned about the singer’s health since she postponed shows last year. So some were thrilled to catch a glimpse of her on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Landon Edwards for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans wait in line to place an order at Dolly’s Tennessean Travel Stop’s coffee bar on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Claims He Asked Prosecutor to ‘Do Me a Favor’ and Look Into the California Election
item.description: The president took credit for the advance of the former Fox News host, Steve Hilton, to the runoff for governor.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump claimed that a call he made to a U.S. attorney in California helped Steve Hilton advance to the runoff for governor. Mr. Hilton said he had no knowledge of any such call.

item.title: California’s Plastic Law Goes to Court: Is It Too Strict or Too Lax?
item.description: The sweeping legislation, fully enacted last month, has been challenged by Republican-led states. Environmental groups have their own complaints.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Rich Pedroncelli/Associated Press
item.media:description: California’s law requires that manufacturers reduce the amount of plastic in single-use items such as shampoo bottles by reducing package sizing, switching to a different material or making the products reusable.

item.title: 59% of Americans say they lack confidence in the Iran deal.
item.description: When it comes to the deal, there is a political divide, according to a new Quinnipiac University poll.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Democratic Leaders Want the Party to Moderate. Its Base Has Other Ideas.
item.description: The primary victories of leftist Democratic congressional candidates in New York came as some leaders were urging the party to move to the center to broaden its appeal.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brad Lander, Claire Valdez, Bernie Sanders, Zohran Mamdani and Darializa Avila Chevalier at a rally earlier this month in New York City.

item.title: Los Angeles Names a New Schools Superintendent After Scandal
item.description: Andres Chait is a district veteran who has helped keep peace with labor unions. He started in the classroom and now runs a district with hundreds of thousands of students.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: via Los Angeles Unified School District
item.media:description: Andres Chait began his career as an elementary teacher and worked his way to the top.

item.title: Federal Appeals Panel Rejects Trump’s Effort to Gather Voting Data From States
item.description: The ruling of a three-judge appeals panel in Michigan was the most significant rebuke yet to the Department of Justice’s effort to find ineligible voters in state voter rolls.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump canceled a bill signing on Thursday to pressure Republicans to pass a voting bill that critics say would make it harder for eligible voters to cast ballots.

item.title: Trump Clashes With Senate Republicans Over Iran in Heated Closed-Door Meeting
item.description: The president had a lengthy and angry exchange with Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana and lit into other Republicans who voted to check his war powers.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana at the Capitol on Wednesday, ahead of a meeting with President Trump and other Senate Republicans.

item.title: Trump Asks Congress for $88 Billion, Mostly for War With Iran
item.description: The proposal was all but dead on arrival in the Senate, where it would need bipartisan support, and comes amid growing G.O.P. skepticism about the conflict.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has met privately with Republicans recently to make the case that the Pentagon urgently needed funding to buy additional weapons.

item.title: Millions of Bees Are Unleashed, and Recaptured, After Truck Flips in Texas
item.description: Local beekeepers rushed to the rescue after a truck carrying roughly 20 million bees overturned on a wet country road in rural Texas.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman

item.title: Postmaster General Confirms Plan to Hold Back Mail Ballots Under Proposed Rule
item.description: The postmaster general, David Steiner, said on Wednesday that under a proposed rule, his agency would not deliver mail ballots in states that decline to hand over voter data.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.category: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: A worker sorting ballots in 2025.

item.title: The World Cup’s Best Party Is Raging in Miami
item.description: It’s Brazil versus Scotland in the South Florida sun.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soccer cognoscenti studying the 2026 World Cup schedule had eyed Wednesday evening’s match between Scotland and Brazil for its revelry potential.

item.title: Pete Hegseth Forces Christopher T. Donahue, a Top Prospect for Army Chief, to Resign
item.description: Gen. Christopher T. Donahue was seen as a rising star in the Army. But Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth viewed him with skepticism.
item.dc:creator: Adam Entous and Greg Jaffe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Allison Joyce/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Gen. Christopher T. Donahue greeting members of the 82nd Airborne Division in 2023.

item.title: Visitors Look at the Reflecting Pool and Disagree on What They See
item.description: People have long gathered in solidarity by the Reflecting Pool, but amid the turmoil of President Trump’s attempted repairs there is little unity to be found.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The changing color of the water has been the subject of curiosity and controversy over the last two weeks.

item.title: From ‘Terrible People’ to ‘Smart People’: The Trump-Led Right Rethinks Iran
item.description: The president has sought to recast the Iranian government as he pursues a peace deal. But there are signs that a softening on Iran in the Republican Party goes well beyond him.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran last month.

item.title: Sergeant Who Shot 5 at Fort Stewart Sentenced to Life in Military Prison
item.description: The sergeant was convicted of attempted murder in the shootings in August at the Georgia base. The attacks stemmed from a domestic dispute, the Army said.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Russ Bynum/Associated Press
item.media:description: An Army sergeant was sentenced to life in a military prison on Tuesday after he was convicted of attempted murder in the shooting of five people at an Army post in Georgia last year.

item.title: What Alex Bores’s New York Defeat Reveals About the Fight Over A.I. Money in Politics
item.description: Alex Bores’s close loss in New York could pave the way for other Democrats to take political advantage of being attacked by the increasingly unpopular A.I. industry.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Laura Brett/Getty Images
item.media:description: Alex Bores with his wife, Darya Moldavskaya, on Tuesday night after he conceded his Democratic primary race for the House in New York City.

item.title: Mayor’s Race in South Dakota’s Largest City Rests on 2 Votes
item.description: With nearly all votes counted, a former Republican legislator was leading a Democratic state senator to be mayor of Sioux Falls, S.D.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Samantha Laurey/Argus Leader/USA Today Network, via Imagn Images
item.media:description: Christine Erickson, a candidate for mayor of Sioux Falls, S.D., at a debate earlier this month.

item.title: Support Builds on the Right for Prosecuting Women Who Get Abortions
item.description: Sentiment is shifting amid frustration in the antiabortion movement that more abortions are happening now than when Roe v. Wade fell.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Kitchener
item.media:credit: Michael Wyke/Houston Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Seth Gruber, an antiabortion activist who leads a group called the White Rose Resistance, has been circulating a petition among other activists to garner support for criminally charging women who get abortions.

item.title: After New York’s Primary Elections, Democrats Face Five Big Questions
item.description: The anti-establishment left surged. How shaken are party leaders?
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York

item.title: Utah Senate President Loses Republican Primary After Data Center Backlash
item.description: The upset of J. Stuart Adams represented one of the strongest examples of how the data center issue has affected elections.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Niki Chan Wylie for The New York Times
item.media:description: J. Stuart Adams, president of the Utah State Senate, in Salt Lake City in 2020.

item.title: With a Proposed $250 Bill, Trump Reimagines America by Putting Himself on Its Money
item.description: Cash might be a relic, but — like royalty and emperors — President Trump still wants his face on American currency.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport

item.title: Pulte Trims Spy Agency, but Spares Bulk of Staff for Now
item.description: Six intelligence officials were fired and nearly four dozen others were sent back to their home agencies elsewhere, former officials said.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Brendan Smialowski/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Current and former officials said Bill Pulte, the acting director of national intelligence, has considered much deeper cuts and bigger structural changes.

item.title: As Blockbusters Loom, Monkey Business at the Supreme Court
item.description: An unlikely trio of justices issued a slashing critique of plea bargains that included several references to orangutans.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court still has yet to decide on several major cases, as its summer recess draws near.

item.title: Trump Refuses to Sign Housing Bill Until Divisive ‘SAVE’ Voting Act Passes Congress
item.description: The president’s announcement declared the bill to be “of minor importance” at the same time that House Republican leaders were heralding it as a major accomplishment.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold, Tony Romm, Tyler Pager and Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The president canceled his plans on Wednesday to sign the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act.

item.title: Calling Democrats ‘Communists,’ Trump Keeps Close Eye on N.Y. Primaries
item.description: The president posted repeatedly into Wednesday morning to label Democrats as extreme, delight in his rivals’ losses and express some apparent envy of Mayor Zohran Mamdani.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has developed a relatively friendly rapport with Mayor Zohran Mamdani, despite their vast differences.

item.title: Camp Mystic, Where 28 Died in Texas Floods, Files for Bankruptcy
item.description: Catastrophic floods that swept the Texas Hill Country last July killed 25 campers, two counselors and the camp’s executive director.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Camp Mystic in December 2025.

item.title: Trump Stokes Chaos in Congress as He Huddles With the G.O.P.
item.description: Hours before visiting the Capitol, the president scrapped plans to sign a major housing bill, condemning “bad Republicans” for resisting his demands to ram through new voting restrictions.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Those attending the meeting at the Capitol on Wednesday said President Trump made a series of highly personal attacks against Republican senators who have bucked him.

item.title: Victories by Pro-Palestinian Democrats Show the Party’s Shift on Israel
item.description: Three Democrats who have been outspoken in their criticism of Israel won primaries in New York on Tuesday, signaling their party’s new skepticism of the country and its actions.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Lexi Parra/The New York Times
item.media:description: Darializa Avila Chevalier, who was endorsed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York City, won an upset Democratic primary victory on Tuesday night over Representative Adriano Espaillat.

item.title: ‘Crazy.’ ‘Ill-Conceived.’ Inside a Scalding World Cup Match.
item.description: One of the hottest games in World Cup history took place in Orlando, Fla., in June 1994, when Mexico took on Ireland. The players look back on it in disbelief.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Alain Gadoffre/Onze/Icon Sport, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mexico defeated Ireland 2-1 after the extreme heat and humidity hit the Irish especially hard.

item.title: Lions, Tigers, Bears and Bomb Sniffing Dogs: Zoos Face Scores of Swatting Attacks
item.description: About 40 zoos and aquariums across the United States have responded to bombing and shooting threats in recent months that turned out to be hoaxes.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: After the swatting episodes, Zoo Miami spent $1 million to update its security cameras and paid the Miami-Dade Sheriff’s Office for a security detail.

item.title: Gay Marriage Is Dividing Republicans, Again
item.description: A look at the backlash to same-sex marriage — its strength, seriousness and the reasons behind it.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:credit: Zach Gibson/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court established same-sex marriage as a national right in 2015.
